title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
A tiered, cohort-based curriculum bridging traditional clinical statistics and modern data science for anesthesiology residents. Built around reproducibility, causal reasoning, and real clinical datasets.
This curriculum is not a generic data science course. Every concept is anchored to anesthesia and critical care practice. Residents leave as researchers who can build, or builders who can publish.

title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
A tiered, cohort-based curriculum bridging traditional clinical statistics and modern data science for anesthesiology residents. Built around reproducibility, causal reasoning, and real clinical datasets.
This curriculum is not a generic data science course. Every concept is anchored to anesthesia and critical care practice. Residents leave as researchers who can build, or builders who can publish.

title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
Curriculum Structure



Tier
Duration
Language
Goal




Introductory
4 weeks
R (toolchain only)
Shared competence: R, RStudio, Git, GitHub, Quarto


Basic
16 weeks
R
Critical consumer + reproducible researcher


Intermediate
20 weeks
Python
Independent analyst, researcher/builder entry


Advanced
24 weeks
Python + AI-assisted
Clinical tool builder or preprint-ready researcher



Progression: Linear. Introductory → Basic → Intermediate → Advanced.
Cohort size: 4–6 residents per cohort.
Cadence: Rolling. All three tiers run concurrently at steady state.
Contact hours: 2 hours/week.
Assessment: Capstone project per tier.

title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
Capstone Deliverables

Introductory — No formal capstone. Completion verified via facilitator checklist.
Basic — Quarto report reproducing and critiquing a published anesthesia paper + R code on GitHub
Intermediate — Quarto manuscript + reproducible Python pipeline using VitalDB or eICU
Advanced — Deployed clinical tool prototype or preprint-ready manuscript (builder/researcher track)

title: Week 3 — R + Quarto Orientation
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Open RStudio and navigate its interface
Create a new Quarto document
Write text, code chunks, and YAML metadata
Render a document to HTML
Push your rendered work to GitHub

title: Week 3 — R + Quarto Orientation
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Open RStudio and navigate its interface
Create a new Quarto document
Write text, code chunks, and YAML metadata
Render a document to HTML
Push your rendered work to GitHub

title: Week 3 — R + Quarto Orientation
text:
Why R + Quarto?

R
R is the lingua franca of clinical research. Most anesthesia papers you read were analyzed with R. Learning R means you can read and reproduce those papers.


Quarto
Quarto is literate programming — code and prose in one document. It ensures:

Your analysis is reproducible (anyone can re-run it)
Your code is documented (explain what you’re doing)
Your workflow is version controlled (Git tracks changes)

title: Week 3 — R + Quarto Orientation
text:
Core Concepts

The Quarto Document Structure
---
title: "My Analysis"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-15"
---

## Introduction

This is **markdown** text.

```r
# This is an R code chunk
mean(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5))
```

## Results

The mean is 3. *(In your document: use an inline R expression here)*


Code Chunks
Three backticks + {r} starts a code chunk. Three backticks ends it.



Chunk Option
Purpose




echo: true
Show code in output (default)


echo: false
Hide code, show output only


eval: true
Run the code (default)


eval: false
Don’t run the code


warning: false
Suppress warnings

title: Week 3 — R + Quarto Orientation
text:
Exercise

Part 1: Create a Quarto Document

Open RStudio
File → New File → Quarto Document
Title: “My First Analysis”
Author: Your name
Save as: ~/mds-work/week3-practice.qmd



Part 2: Write Content
Replace the template with (copy exactly, including the backticks that start/end code chunks):
---
title: "Drug Dose Calculator"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-15"
---

## Setup

Load the packages we need:

```r
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
```

## Data

Create a simple dataset of patient weights:

```r
patients <- tibble(
  id = 1:5,
  weight_kg = c(70, 85, 60, 90, 75)
)

patients
```

## Calculation

Calculate propofol induction dose (2 mg/kg):

```r
patients <- patients |>
  mutate(propofol_mg = weight_kg * 2)

patients
```

## Summary

The mean propofol dose is 310 mg. *(In your document: use an inline R expression here)*


Part 3: Render

Click “Render” (or press Ctrl+Shift+K / Cmd+Shift+K)
View the HTML output
Notice how code, output, and text are interwoven



Part 4: Version Control
Your mds-work folder should already have Git initialized from Week 2. If not:

Initialize Git in your mds-work folder (skip if already done):
cd ~/mds-work
git init
Add and commit your Week 3 file:
git add week3-practice.qmd
git commit -m "Add Week 3 practice document"
Push to your personal GitHub repository (create one called mds-work if you haven’t yet)

title: Week 3 — R + Quarto Orientation
text:
R Basics in 5 Minutes

Variables
patient_weight <- 70  # Assign
patient_weight        # Print


Vectors
weights <- c(70, 85, 60, 90, 75)
mean(weights)
sd(weights)


Functions
round(3.14159, 2)  # 3.14
sqrt(16)           # 4

title: Week 3 — R + Quarto Orientation
text:
Common Pitfalls







Problem
Solution




“Object not found”
Check spelling; ensure you ran the code chunk


Chunk won’t run
Check that backticks are correct (three, not fancy quotes)


Package not found
Run install.packages("packagename") once


Render fails
Check YAML indentation (spaces, not tabs)

title: Week 3 — R + Quarto Orientation
text:
Verification
Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

Create a new Quarto document in RStudio
Write text, code, and inline code
Render to HTML

Push your work to GitHub

title: Week 1 — Command Line Basics

title: Week 1 — Command Line Basics
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Open a terminal (Terminal on macOS/Linux, Git Bash on Windows)
Navigate the file system using cd, ls/dir, and pwd
Create directories with mkdir
Create, move, and rename files
Understand absolute vs relative paths

title: Week 1 — Command Line Basics
text:
Why the Command Line?
The command line is the universal interface to your computer. Every tool we use — R, Python, Git, Quarto — can be controlled via the command line. Learning it early means you can troubleshoot any tool later.

title: Week 1 — Command Line Basics
text:
Core Concepts

The Terminal
The terminal is a text-based interface to your computer. You type commands; the computer responds.


File System Navigation



Command
Purpose
Example




pwd
Print working directory (where am I?)
pwd


ls
List files in current directory
ls or ls -la


cd
Change directory
cd Documents


cd ..
Go up one directory
cd ..


cd ~
Go to home directory
cd ~





File Operations



Command
Purpose
Example




mkdir
Make directory
mkdir my-project


touch
Create empty file
touch notes.txt


mv
Move or rename
mv old.txt new.txt


cp
Copy
cp file.txt backup.txt


rm
Remove file
rm file.txt


rm -r
Remove directory
rm -r my-folder

title: Week 1 — Command Line Basics
text:
Exercise

Part 1: Navigation

Open your terminal
Type pwd — note the full path
Type ls — see what files/folders are here
Type cd ~ — go to home directory
Type pwd again — confirm you’re at home



Part 2: File Operations

Create a directory: mkdir mds-intro
Enter it: cd mds-intro
Create a file: touch notes.txt (macOS/Linux) or type nul > notes.txt (Windows)
List it: ls — should see notes.txt
Rename it: mv notes.txt week1-notes.txt
Create a subdirectory: mkdir exercises
Move the file into it: mv week1-notes.txt exercises/
Navigate in: cd exercises
List: ls — should see your file



Part 3: Relative vs Absolute Paths
From ~/mds-intro/exercises/, try:

pwd — shows absolute path
cd .. — goes up to mds-intro
cd ../.. — goes up two levels
cd ~/mds-intro/exercises — absolute path back

title: Week 1 — Command Line Basics
text:
Common Pitfalls








Problem
Cause
Solution




“Command not found”
Typo or not in PATH
Check spelling; ensure tool is installed


“No such file”
Wrong path
Use pwd to see where you are; use ls to see files


Spaces in names
Unescaped spaces
Quote paths: cd "My Folder" or escape: cd My\ Folder

title: Week 1 — Command Line Basics
text:
Verification
Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

Open terminal independently
Navigate to any directory using cd
Create a folder and a file
Move and rename files
Explain the difference between cd .. and cd ~

text: The Intermediate tier transitions residents from reproduction to independent analysis. Introduction to prediction vs inference as the central methodological distinction.

title: Intermediate Tier
text: The Intermediate tier transitions residents from reproduction to independent analysis. Introduction to prediction vs inference as the central methodological distinction.

title: Intermediate Tier
text:
Why Python?
The builder track opens at Intermediate. EHR wrangling, feature engineering, and prediction pipelines are better served by Python’s ecosystem. scikit-learn, lifelines, polars, shap — no equivalent coherence in R for this workflow.

eICU-CRD: PhysioNet credentialed critical care database

title: Basic Tier — Verification Checklist
text: This checklist verifies that every resident’s environment is functional before Week 1 begins. The Basic tier requires working R, packages, Quarto, and GitHub connectivity. Do not proceed with content delivery until all items are checked. A resident with a broken environment will fall behind permanently.

title: Basic Tier — Verification Checklist

Do not proceed with content delivery until all items are checked. A resident with a broken environment will fall behind permanently.

title: Basic Tier — Verification Checklist
text:
Individual Resident Verification
Resident Name: _________________ Date: _________ Verified By: _________________

R Installation

Can open RStudio
Can execute R --version in terminal and reports 4.3.0 or newer
Can install a package: install.packages("tidyverse")
Package loads without error: library(tidyverse)



Essential Packages

library(tableone) loads without error
library(survival) loads without error
library(survminer) loads without error



Git Installation

Can open Terminal (macOS/Linux) or Git Bash (Windows)
Can execute git --version and reports 2.40.0 or newer
Can execute git config --global user.name and see their name
Can execute git config --global user.email and see their email



GitHub Connectivity

Has GitHub account with 2FA enabled
Can execute ssh -T git@github.com and see “Hi username! You’ve successfully authenticated”
Can clone a repository: git clone git@github.com:balinesthesia/mds-curriculum.git
Can navigate into cloned directory and see files



Quarto Installation

Can execute quarto --version and reports 1.4.0 or newer
Can render a basic document (create test.qmd with one heading, render to HTML)
HTML output opens in browser without errors



RStudio Integration

Can create a new Quarto Document from RStudio menu
Can render document using Render button
Git tab shows in RStudio Environment pane (after opening Git project)
Can commit a file change from RStudio Git pane



File System Navigation

Can open terminal in a specific directory
Can navigate up/down directory tree (cd, cd .., ls/dir)
Can create a new directory: mkdir test-folder
Can create a new file: touch test.txt (macOS/Linux) or type nul > test.txt (Windows CMD)
Can move and rename files

title: Basic Tier — Verification Checklist
text:
Failure Protocols

If a resident fails verification:

Immediate: Schedule 1-on-1 session within 48 hours
During session: Walk through failed items together
If unresolved: Provide temporary cloud environment (GitHub Codespaces) as bridge
Document: Log issue and resolution for future cohorts



If >20% of cohort fails verification:

Do not start Week 1
Reschedule: Move Week 1 by 1 week
Root cause: Identify common failure mode (usually Windows Git, SSH, or package installation)
Remediation: Group session targeting specific failure

title: Datasets
text:
Curated synthetic and public anesthesia datasets
Small, clean, immediately usable
Committed directly to repository





VitalDB: Open access, committed if <100MB
eICU-CRD: PhysioNet credentialed, samples committed if <100MB
Larger datasets: Pointers and download instructions





MIMIC-IV: Never committed to repository
Modules assume local data/mimic-iv/ directory
Each module includes data/README.md specifying exact tables, columns, and cohort query
Reproducibility via code, not data distribution

title: Week 1 — Environment & Philosophy
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain why reproducibility matters in clinical research
Distinguish between reproducibility, repeatability, and replicability
Identify common methodological failures that lead to retraction
Use Retraction Watch to find case studies of failed research
Recognize the hallmarks of irreproducible anesthesia research

title: Week 1 — Environment & Philosophy
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain why reproducibility matters in clinical research
Distinguish between reproducibility, repeatability, and replicability
Identify common methodological failures that lead to retraction
Use Retraction Watch to find case studies of failed research
Recognize the hallmarks of irreproducible anesthesia research

title: Week 1 — Environment & Philosophy
text:
Why Reproducibility Matters
Anesthesia practice depends on evidence. When we choose an induction dose, a monitoring technique, or a rescue protocol, we assume the evidence base is sound. But evidence fails when research cannot be reproduced.
Clinical stakes: Irreproducible findings enter guidelines. Incorrect guidelines harm patients. Retractions damage trust in our specialty.
Your role: As a clinician who reads and contributes to the literature, you are a gatekeeper. Learn to recognize weak evidence before it shapes your practice.

title: Week 1 — Environment & Philosophy
text:
Core Concepts

Definitions








Term
Meaning
Example




Repeatability
Same team, same setup, same result
Lab reruns the analysis; gets identical coefficients


Reproducibility
Different team, same data, same result
Independent analyst downloads your dataset; recreates your figures


Replicability
Different team, different data, similar result
New study in different hospital confirms your finding



We focus on reproducibility in this tier. It is the foundation — without it, replicability is meaningless.


The Reproducibility Crisis
Estimates suggest 50–90% of published research findings cannot be reproduced. Causes include:

Unavailable data or code — the “trust me” approach
P-hacking — running multiple analyses, reporting only significant ones
HARKing — Hypothesizing After Results are Known
Statistical errors — incorrect tests, multiple comparison failures
Fraud — fabrication, falsification (rare but catastrophic)



Retraction Watch
Retraction Watch (retractionwatch.com) maintains a database of retracted papers with reasons. It is a teaching tool: every retraction contains a lesson.
Common reasons in anesthesia literature:



Reason
Example Pattern




Data fabrication
Patient records do not match hospital database


Statistical error
Incorrect analysis chosen; p-values invalid


Unavailable data
Authors decline to share raw data upon request


Ethical violation
Lack of IRB approval for human subjects


Image manipulation
Western blot bands duplicated or altered

title: Week 1 — Environment & Philosophy
text:
Exercise

Part 1: Explore Retraction Watch

Go to retractionwatch.com
Search for “anesthesia” or “anesthesiology”
Select one retracted paper from a mainstream journal (Anesthesia & Analgesia, British Journal of Anaesthesia, Anesthesiology)
Read the retraction notice (usually linked)
Note: year of publication, year of retraction, stated reason



Part 2: Identify the Failure
For your selected paper, answer:

What was the core claim? (What did the authors say they found?)
What methodological failure led to retraction?
Was the error detectable from the published paper alone?
Could sharing raw data or code have prevented this?



Part 3: Reflect
Write 3–5 sentences:

What warning signs might a critical reader have noticed before the retraction? What does this teach us about our responsibility as consumers of anesthesia research?

Save your response as week-01-reflection.md in your course repository.

title: Week 1 — Environment & Philosophy
text:
Common Pitfalls in Anesthesia Research








Pitfall
Why It Happens
How to Spot It




Underpowered study
Small N, rare outcomes
Confidence intervals include null; post-hoc power calculations


Multiple comparisons unchecked
Many secondary outcomes
No Bonferroni, FDR, or other adjustment; p-values cluster near 0.05


Selective reporting
Negative primary outcome
Protocol pre-specified outcomes differ from published outcomes


Confounding by indication
Retrospective drug comparison
Groups differ systematically (sicker patients get drug X)


Missing data mishandling
Dropouts, incomplete records
No description of missingness; complete-case analysis only

title: Week 1 — Environment & Philosophy
text:
Verification
Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

Navigate Retraction Watch and find anesthesia-related retractions
Identify the stated reason for a retraction
Explain the difference between reproducibility and replicability
List three warning signs of potentially irreproducible research
Submit your reflection document

title: Week 1 — Environment & Philosophy
text:
Resources

Retraction Watch Database: retractionwatch.com
Paper: Ioannidis, J.P.A. (2005). “Why Most Published Research Findings Are False.” PLoS Medicine
Blog: Error Statistics Philosophy — Deborah Mayo on statistical criticism
Book: Rigor Mortis (Richard Harris) — case studies of failed biomedical research


← Back to Basic Tier

title: Advanced Tier
text: The Advanced tier produces residents who can build clinical tools or produce preprint-ready research. AI-assisted workflows are taught critically — residents learn what LLMs cannot catch before they learn to use them.

title: Advanced Tier
text: The Advanced tier produces residents who can build clinical tools or produce preprint-ready research. AI-assisted workflows are taught critically — residents learn what LLMs cannot catch before they learn to use them.

title: Advanced Tier
text:
Why AI-assisted?
By Advanced, residents have enough foundation to use AI tools critically — as a supervised junior collaborator, not an oracle. Teaching AI-assisted workflows without this foundation produces tool dependency, not competency.

title: Advanced Tier
text:
Datasets

MIMIC-IV: PhysioNet credentialed (local copy required, never committed)

title: Advanced Tier
text:
Capstone
Deployed clinical tool prototype or preprint-ready manuscript (builder/researcher track).

title: Advanced Tier
text:
Facilitator Pipeline
Advanced graduates can become junior facilitators for Basic cohorts, creating a sustainable curriculum model.

title: Advanced Tier
text:
Modules
Coming soon. Module structure: Week 1–24 with index.qmd (resident-facing) and facilitator.qmd (facilitator notes).

← Back to Home

title: Basic Tier
text: The Basic tier transforms clinicians into reproducible researchers. Residents learn to read, critique, and reproduce the statistical methods they encounter in anesthesia literature.

title: Basic Tier
text: The Basic tier transforms clinicians into reproducible researchers. Residents learn to read, critique, and reproduce the statistical methods they encounter in anesthesia literature.

title: Basic Tier
text:
Why R?
Clinical literature residents read is predominantly R-generated (tableone, survival, ggplot2). Teaching R at Basic means they can read and reproduce the code in papers they cite within weeks. Immediate clinical payoff drives retention.

title: Basic Tier
text:
Capstone
Quarto report reproducing and critiquing a published anesthesia paper + R code on GitHub.

title: Basic Tier
text:
Modules
Coming soon. Module structure: Week 1–16 with index.qmd (resident-facing) and facilitator.qmd (facilitator notes).

← Back to Home

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain why literate programming prevents the failures you analyzed in Week 1
Create a reproducible Quarto document with code, prose, and metadata
Use chunk options to control what is shown vs. what is executed
Render a document to HTML and verify it reproduces without error
Push a complete analysis to GitHub as a version-controlled artifact

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain why literate programming prevents the failures you analyzed in Week 1
Create a reproducible Quarto document with code, prose, and metadata
Use chunk options to control what is shown vs. what is executed
Render a document to HTML and verify it reproduces without error
Push a complete analysis to GitHub as a version-controlled artifact

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Why Literate Programming?
In Week 1, you analyzed retracted papers. One recurring failure mode was: the analysis could not be reproduced because the method was described in prose, not in code. Readers had to trust the authors’ verbal description of their statistical procedure.
Literate programming (Quarto, R Markdown, Jupyter) inverts this. The code is the method description. The prose explains why. Together they create a single artifact that:

Can be re-run by anyone with the data
Documents decisions where they were made (in the same file)
Is version-controlled, so every change is tracked

This is not a convenience. It is a disciplinary practice that protects against the exact failures you found in Retraction Watch.

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Core Concepts

The Reproducibility Stack
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  Quarto document (.qmd) │  ← Code + prose + metadata
├─────────────────────────┤
│  Version control (Git)  │  ← Every change tracked
├─────────────────────────┤
│  Remote repository        │  ← GitHub: backup + share
├─────────────────────────┤
│  Environment (renv)     │  ← Package versions locked
└─────────────────────────┘
Week 2 covers the top two layers. Environment management (renv) comes later.


Chunk Options for Reproducibility








Option
Purpose
Reproducibility Role




echo: true
Show code
Others can see exactly what was done


echo: false
Hide code
Clean output, but code is still in source


eval: true
Run code
Default; ensures output is current


eval: false
Skip execution
For slow or one-time operations


message: false
Hide package messages
Cleaner output without losing code


warning: false
Hide warnings
Use carefully — warnings often matter



Rule: echo: true for all analysis code unless you have a specific reason. Transparency is the default.


YAML Metadata as Documentation
The YAML header is not decoration. It is the minimum viable documentation for reproducibility:
---
title: "Propofol Dose Analysis"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-22"
format: html
---
Add these fields for research documents:
---
title: "Propofol Dose Analysis"
subtitle: "Basic Tier — Week 2 Exercise"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-22"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false    # Never hide code by default
    toc: true         # Table of contents for navigation
execute:
  echo: true          # Global echo setting
---

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Exercise

Part 1: Create a Reproducible Document

Open RStudio
File → New File → Quarto Document
Save as: ~/mds-work/basic-week2-reproducibility.qmd
Replace the template with the following structure (copy exactly):

---
title: "Reproducibility Exercise — Basic Week 2"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-22"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    toc: true
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Purpose

This document demonstrates a reproducible analysis workflow. Every step is documented in code and prose.

## Data

Load the built-in `mtcars` dataset (used here as a stand-in for clinical data):

```r
#| message: false
data(mtcars)
head(mtcars)

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Analysis
Calculate mean miles per gallon (analogous to calculating mean drug dose):
mean_mpg <- mean(mtcars$mpg)
mean_mpg

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Results
The mean MPG is r round(mean_mpg, 1).

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Conclusion
This analysis is fully reproducible: the code is visible, the data source is named, and the output is generated from the code.

### Part 2: Render and Verify

1. Click **Render** (or Ctrl+Shift+K / Cmd+Shift+K)
2. Open the generated HTML file in a browser
3. Verify:
   - [ ] Code is visible for every analysis step
   - [ ] Output appears below each code chunk
   - [ ] No "magic numbers" — every statistic is generated from code
   - [ ] The document renders without errors

### Part 3: Version Control

1. Ensure your `mds-work` folder is a Git repository (initialized in Intro Week 2):
   ```bash
   cd ~/mds-work
   git status

Stage, commit, and push:
git add basic-week2-reproducibility.qmd
git commit -m "Add Week 2 reproducibility exercise"
git push origin main
Verify on GitHub that the .qmd file is visible.


Part 4: Reflection
In your document, add a final section:
## Reflection

How does this workflow prevent the failures you found in Retraction Watch? Write 2–3 sentences.
Re-render and push the updated document.

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Common Pitfalls








Pitfall
Why It Happens
How to Prevent




“It works on my machine”
Undocumented package versions
Use renv (covered later); for now, note package versions in prose


“I changed the data but forgot to update the text”
Manual text updates
Use inline R expressions: `r mean(mtcars$mpg)`


“The code is in the document but I can’t find it”
echo: false everywhere
Default to echo: true; hide code only for final reports


“I rendered once and never again”
Stale output
Re-render before every commit; check that HTML updates


“I pushed the HTML but not the QMD”
Only committing output
Always commit .qmd (source); HTML is generated, optional to commit

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Verification
Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

Explain how literate programming prevents irreproducible analysis
Create a Quarto document with YAML, prose, code chunks, and inline expressions
Use echo: true as the default chunk option
Render to HTML and verify the output
Commit and push both .qmd and .html to GitHub
Identify one Retraction Watch failure mode that this workflow would have prevented

title: Week 2 — Literate Programming for Reproducible Research
text:
Resources

Quarto Guide: quarto.org/docs/guide
R for Data Science (2e): r4ds.hadley.nz — Quarto chapter
Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Computational Research: PLOS Computational Biology

title: Introductory Course — Setup Guide
text: This guide ensures every resident arrives at Week 1 with a working development environment. Do not assume prior technical knowledge. Test this process yourself on a clean machine before the cohort begins.

title: Introductory Course — Setup Guide
text: This guide ensures every resident arrives at Week 1 with a working development environment. Do not assume prior technical knowledge. Test this process yourself on a clean machine before the cohort begins.

title: Introductory Course — Setup Guide
text:
OS Requirements



OS
Minimum Version
Notes




Windows
Windows 10
WSL2 recommended for Git Bash compatibility


macOS
macOS 11 (Big Sur)
Terminal.app or iTerm2


Linux
Ubuntu 20.04 LTS
Any modern distro with apt equivalent

title: Introductory Course — Setup Guide
text:
Installation Steps

1. R (4.3.0 or newer)
Windows: 1. Download from cran.r-project.org 2. Run installer with default options 3. Verify: Open RGui, type R.version.string
macOS:
brew install --cask r

Note: The --cask flag installs the official CRAN R with full toolchain support needed for package compilation.

Linux (Ubuntu):
sudo apt update
sudo apt install r-base


2. RStudio or VSCode
RStudio (recommended for beginners): - Download from posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/
VSCode (alternative): - Download from code.visualstudio.com - Install extensions: R Extension Pack, Quarto


3. Quarto
Download from quarto.org/docs/get-started
Verify installation:
quarto --version


4. Git
Windows: - Download from git-scm.com - Use Git Bash as terminal
macOS:
xcode-select --install
Linux:
sudo apt install git
Verify:
git --version


5. GitHub Account

Create account at github.com
Enable two-factor authentication
Add SSH key (follow GitHub docs)
Test: ssh -T git@github.com

title: Introductory Course — Setup Guide
text:
Pre-Session Distribution
Send residents this checklist 2 weeks before Week 1:
Subject: Medical Data Science — Pre-Course Setup Required

Dear Resident,

Before our first session, please complete the environment setup:

□ Install R (4.3.0+): https://cran.r-project.org/
□ Install RStudio: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/
□ Install Quarto: https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
□ Install Git: https://git-scm.com/downloads
□ Create GitHub account: https://github.com (enable 2FA)
□ Add SSH key to GitHub (follow their documentation)

Verification: Can you run these commands in Terminal/Git Bash?
  R --version        (Should show R version 4.3.0 or newer)
  git --version
  quarto --version
  ssh -T git@github.com

Reply to this email with "Setup complete" once done.

title: Introductory Course — Setup Guide
text:
Troubleshooting Common Issues







Issue
Resolution




“R not found”
Check PATH; restart RStudio after R installation


Git Bash won’t open
Install Git with “Use Git from Git Bash only” option


SSH key rejected
Ensure key is added to ssh-agent: eval "$(ssh-agent -s)" && ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519


Quarto render fails
Check for spaces in file paths (Windows)

title: Introductory Course — Setup Guide
text:
Facilitator Verification
Before the cohort starts:

Tested setup on clean Windows VM
Tested setup on clean macOS install
Sent pre-session email to all residents
Tracked responses; followed up with non-responders
Scheduled 1-on-1 sessions for residents reporting issues

title: Basic Tier — Setup Guide
text: This guide ensures every resident arrives at Week 1 with a working development environment. The Basic tier requires a complete R ecosystem for reproducible research. Do not assume prior technical knowledge. Test this process yourself on a clean machine before the cohort begins.

text: This guide ensures every resident arrives at Week 1 with a working development environment. The Basic tier requires a complete R ecosystem for reproducible research. Do not assume prior technical knowledge. Test this process yourself on a clean machine before the cohort begins.

title: Basic Tier — Setup Guide
text:
Installation Steps

1. R (4.3.0 or newer)
Windows: 1. Download from cran.r-project.org 2. Run installer with default options 3. Verify: Open RGui, type R.version.string
macOS:
brew install --cask r

Note: The --cask flag installs the official CRAN R with full toolchain support needed for package compilation.

Linux (Ubuntu):
sudo apt update
sudo apt install r-base


2. RStudio Desktop
Download from posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/
RStudio provides an integrated environment with: - Script editor with syntax highlighting - Console for interactive R execution - Environment and file browsers - Built-in Git integration - Quarto rendering support
Alternative: VSCode If you prefer VSCode: - Download from code.visualstudio.com - Install extensions: R Extension Pack, Quarto


3. Essential R Packages
Open RStudio and run in the Console:
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "tableone",
  "survival",
  "survminer",
  "ggplot2",
  "dplyr",
  "readr",
  "knitr",
  "rmarkdown"
))
Verify installation:
library(tidyverse)
library(survival)


4. Quarto
Download from quarto.org/docs/get-started
Verify installation:
quarto --version


5. Git
Windows: - Download from git-scm.com - Use Git Bash as terminal
macOS:
xcode-select --install
Linux:
sudo apt install git
Verify:
git --version


6. GitHub Account

Create account at github.com
Enable two-factor authentication
Add SSH key (follow GitHub docs)
Test: ssh -T git@github.com

title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
Duration: 4 weeks
Goal: Zero-assumption entry path — establish shared toolchain competence before Basic tier content begins
Residents arrive with clinical intelligence but no programming or statistics background. The Introductory Course separates tool learning from concept learning. Without this foundation, Week 1 of Basic would require simultaneous learning of tools and concepts — a proven failure mode.

title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
Duration: 4 weeks
Goal: Zero-assumption entry path — establish shared toolchain competence before Basic tier content begins
Residents arrive with clinical intelligence but no programming or statistics background. The Introductory Course separates tool learning from concept learning. Without this foundation, Week 1 of Basic would require simultaneous learning of tools and concepts — a proven failure mode.

title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
Learning Goals
By the end of this course, residents will:

Navigate the command line confidently (terminal, file system, paths)
Use Git and GitHub for version control (clone, commit, push, fork)
Render a Quarto document with R code chunks
Understand core programming concepts (variables, data types, functions)
Refresh basic statistics intuition (mean, median, variance, distributions, p-values)

2

title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
Prerequisites

Clinical intelligence (you have this)
No programming or statistics background assumed
Personal laptop (Windows, macOS, or Linux)

title: Medical Data Science Curriculum
text:
Full Curriculum
The Introductory Course is the entry point to a four-tier curriculum. See the Curriculum Overview for the complete structure: Basic → Intermediate → Advanced.
Code

Overview
Residents arrive with clinical intelligence but no programming or statistics background. The Introductory Course separates tool learning from concept learning. Without this foundation, Week 1 of Basic would require simultaneous learning of tools and concepts — a proven failure mode.

Learning Goals
By the end of this course, residents will:

Navigate the command line confidently (terminal, file system, paths)
Use Git and GitHub for version control (clone, commit, push, fork)
Render a Quarto document with R code chunks
Understand core programming concepts (variables, data types, functions)
Refresh basic statistics intuition (mean, median, variance, distributions, p-values)

Structure
Each week follows the pattern:
week-NN/
├── index.qmd          # Resident-facing module
├── facilitator.qmd    # Facilitator notes
└── data/              # Module dataset (if any)

Week Overview








Week
Topic
Key Skills




1
Command line basics
Terminal navigation, file operations


2
Git and GitHub
Version control, repository workflow


3
R + Quarto orientation
Literate programming, document rendering


4
Programming + Statistics
Variables, functions, summary statistics

Capstone
No formal capstone. Completion verified via facilitator/intro-verification-checklist.qmd.

Prerequisites

Clinical intelligence (you have this)
No programming or statistics background assumed
Personal laptop (Windows, macOS, or Linux)


See Curriculum Overview for the full tier structure.

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain what version control is and why it matters
Configure Git with your identity
Clone a repository from GitHub
Stage, commit, and push changes
Fork a repository and make a pull request

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain what version control is and why it matters
Configure Git with your identity
Clone a repository from GitHub
Stage, commit, and push changes
Fork a repository and make a pull request

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
Why Version Control?
Clinical data science is collaborative and iterative. Version control lets you:

Track every change to your analysis
Collaborate without overwriting each other’s work
Revert mistakes instantly
Share your work with the world (or keep it private)

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
Core Concepts

Git vs GitHub

Git — Version control software (runs on your computer)
GitHub — Web platform for hosting Git repositories (cloud service)



The Git Workflow
Working Directory → Staging Area → Repository → GitHub
        |                |              |           |
      edit           git add        git commit   git push

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
Setup

Configure Git Identity
Tell Git who you are (one-time setup):
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@hospital.id"
Verify:
git config --global user.name
git config --global user.email


Connect to GitHub
Ensure your SSH key is set up (from Week 1 setup):
ssh -T git@github.com
You should see: “Hi username! You’ve successfully authenticated…”

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
Exercise

Part 1: Clone a Repository

Navigate to your home directory: cd ~
Create a folder for coursework: mkdir mds-work and enter it: cd mds-work
Clone the curriculum repository:
git clone git@github.com:balinesthesia/mds-curriculum.git
Navigate into it: cd mds-curriculum
List files: ls — you should see README.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, etc.
Check Git status: git status — should say “nothing to commit”



Part 2: Make Your First Contribution

Fork the repository on GitHub (click “Fork” on the repo page)
Clone your fork:
cd ~
git clone git@github.com:YOUR-USERNAME/mds-curriculum.git my-fork
cd my-fork
Create a new branch: git checkout -b my-first-edit
Open README.md and add your name to the bottom (as a comment: <!-- Resident: Your Name -->)
Check status: git status — should show README.md as modified
Stage the change: git add README.md
Commit: git commit -m "Add my name to README"
Push: git push origin my-first-edit
Create a Pull Request on GitHub



Part 3: Understanding the Commands



Command
What it does




git status
Shows what’s changed


git add filename
Stages a file for commit


git commit -m "message"
Saves staged changes with a message


git push
Sends commits to GitHub


git pull
Downloads changes from GitHub


git log
Shows commit history

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
Key Principles

Commit Messages
Good commit messages explain why, not just what:

❌ “Update file”
✅ “Fix dose calculation error in propofol analysis”



Atomic Commits
Each commit should represent one logical change:

❌ One commit with “Add data cleaning + fix typo + update README”
✅ Three separate commits

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
Common Pitfalls







Problem
Solution




“Permission denied” on push
Check SSH key is added to ssh-agent


“Merge conflict”
Edit the file to resolve markers, then commit


Forgot to stage a file before committing
git add filename then git commit --amend --no-edit


Pushed sensitive data
Immediately rotate credentials; don’t just delete from Git (history remains)

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
Verification
Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

Configure Git with your name and email
Clone a repository from GitHub
Make an edit, stage, commit, and push
Create a pull request
Explain the difference between git add, git commit, and git push

title: Week 2 — Git and GitHub Fundamentals
text:
Resources

GitHub Git Cheat Sheet
Oh Shit, Git!?! — Common mistake recovery

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Define and use variables, data types, and functions
Read and write CSV files in R
Compute summary statistics (mean, median, SD)
Understand distributions and p-values conceptually
Load data, analyze it, and save results

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Define and use variables, data types, and functions
Read and write CSV files in R
Compute summary statistics (mean, median, SD)
Understand distributions and p-values conceptually
Load data, analyze it, and save results

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
Why This Matters
Clinical data is messy and complex. Programming gives you the tools to:

Process thousands of patient records systematically
Reproduce your analysis exactly
Share your methods transparently

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
Core Concepts

Variables and Data Types



Type
Example
Clinical Example




Numeric
70.5
Patient weight, lab value


Integer
42L
Patient count, age in years


Character
"Male"
Sex, diagnosis code


Logical
TRUE
Event occurred? (yes/no)


Factor
factor("Severe")
Ordered category (ASA I-V)





Data Structures
# Vector (one column)
ages <- c(45, 62, 33, 58, 71)

# Data frame (table)
patients <- data.frame(
  id = 1:5,
  age = ages,
  sex = c("M", "F", "F", "M", "M"),
  bmi = c(24.5, 28.2, 22.1, 31.5, 26.0)
)


Functions
A function takes input, does something, returns output:
# Built-in
mean(patients$age)
sd(patients$bmi)

# Your own
calculate_bmi <- function(weight_kg, height_m) {
  bmi <- weight_kg / (height_m ^ 2)
  return(bmi)
}

calculate_bmi(70, 1.75)  # 22.86

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
File I/O

Reading CSV
# Load data
data <- read_csv("patients.csv")

# View structure
str(data)
head(data)


Writing CSV
# Save results
write_csv(results, "analysis_output.csv")

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
Statistics Refresher (No Code)

Measures of Central Tendency








Measure
When to Use
Example




Mean
Symmetric data, no outliers
Average MAP in stable patients


Median
Skewed data, outliers present
ICU length of stay (some very long stays)


Mode
Categorical data
Most common ASA class





Spread



Measure
Interpretation




SD
Typical distance from mean


IQR
Range of middle 50% of data


Range
Min to max (sensitive to outliers)





Distributions

Normal (Gaussian): Symmetric, bell-shaped (e.g., height in adults)
Skewed right: Tail to the right (e.g., hospital length of stay)
Skewed left: Tail to the left (rare in clinical data)
Bimodal: Two peaks (e.g., blood pressure by sex)



The p-value
A p-value is the probability of seeing your result (or more extreme) if the null hypothesis were true.

Small p (e.g., < 0.05): Unlikely under null → “statistically significant”
Large p (e.g., > 0.05): Plausibly under null → “not significant”

Warning: Statistical significance ≠ clinical importance. A drug can lower blood pressure by 0.5 mmHg with p < 0.001 and still be clinically meaningless.

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
Exercise

Part 1: Load and Explore Data
Create a CSV file ~/mds-work/patients.csv:
id,age,sex,weight_kg,height_m,systolic_bp
1,45,M,70,1.75,120
2,62,F,65,1.60,135
3,33,F,55,1.55,110
4,58,M,85,1.80,145
5,71,M,78,1.70,150
Now in R:
library(tidyverse)

# Load
patients <- read_csv("~/mds-work/patients.csv")

# Explore
str(patients)
summary(patients)


Part 2: Calculate Statistics
# Age statistics
mean(patients$age)
median(patients$age)
sd(patients$age)

# BMI calculation
patients <- patients |>
  mutate(
    bmi = weight_kg / (height_m ^ 2),
    bmi_category = case_when(
      bmi < 18.5 ~ "Underweight",
      bmi < 25 ~ "Normal",
      bmi < 30 ~ "Overweight",
      TRUE ~ "Obese"
    )
  )

# Summary by category
patients |>
  group_by(bmi_category) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_sbp = mean(systolic_bp),
    sd_sbp = sd(systolic_bp)  # NA when n = 1 — expected with small datasets
  )


Part 3: Save Results
# Save processed data
write_csv(patients, "~/mds-work/patients_analyzed.csv")

# Save summary statistics
summary_stats <- patients |>
  summarise(
    across(c(age, bmi, systolic_bp), list(mean = mean, sd = sd, median = median))
  )

write_csv(summary_stats, "~/mds-work/summary_stats.csv")


Part 4: Create a Quarto Report
Create ~/mds-work/week4-report.qmd:
---
title: "Week 4 Analysis Report"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-04-15"
---

## Data Summary

Analyzing N patients.  
Mean age: X years.  
Mean BMI: Y.  
*(Replace N, X, Y with inline R expressions: backtick-r-nrow, backtick-r-mean, etc.)*

## By BMI Category

```r
patients |>
  group_by(bmi_category) |>
  summarise(n = n(), mean_sbp = mean(systolic_bp)) |>
  knitr::kable()
```

## Conclusion

Data processing complete. Files saved to `patients_analyzed.csv`.
Render and push to GitHub.

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
Common Pitfalls







Problem
Solution




“File not found”
Check working directory: getwd(); use full path


Factor vs character confusion
Use stringsAsFactors = FALSE or read_csv()


NA values break calculations
Use na.rm = TRUE: mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)


Wrong variable type
Convert: as.numeric(), as.character()

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
Verification
Before leaving this session, confirm you can:

Create a data frame with multiple variable types
Write a simple function
Read a CSV file and inspect its structure
Calculate mean, median, and SD
Add a new calculated column to data
Save results to CSV
Explain when to use mean vs median

title: Week 4 — Programming Concepts + Statistics Refresher
text:
Resources

R for Data Science: Data transformation
Statistical Rethinking — Conceptual depth (optional)
